title: To promote the development of renewable energy on public land, and for other purposes.
plain_summary: This bill helps build more wind and solar power projects on government land. The government wants to create 60 gigawatts of renewable energy by 2030, which is much more than the old goal of 25 gigawatts. The bill makes it easier and faster to get permission to build these energy projects. Some of the money made from renewable energy will go into a special fund to protect animals and make parks and outdoor areas better for people to visit.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Commodity Markets, Digital Assets, and Rural Development.

sections.title: Section 1 Definitions
sections.text: This section explains what different terms mean throughout this law. Covered land means federal land that is allowed to have wind, solar, or geothermal energy projects built on it according to existing land use plans and federal laws. It does not include areas already covered by California's Desert Renewable Energy Conservation Plan or areas where the Secretary of the Interior decides current planning is already good enough. Energy storage project means equipment that can store at least five kilowatt hours of energy using batteries, compressed air, pumped water, hydrogen, heat storage, fuel cells, flywheels, capacitors, superconducting magnets, or other technologies approved by the Secretary of Energy. Exclusion area means covered land that the Bureau of Land Management says is not suitable for renewable energy projects. Federal land includes both public land managed by the Bureau of Land Management and National Forest System lands managed by the Forest Service where geothermal leases are allowed. The Fund means the Renewable Energy Resource Conservation Fund created by this law. Land use plan means official planning documents created under federal land management laws. Priority area means covered land that the Bureau of Land Management has identified as a preferred location for renewable energy projects. Renewable energy project means a project on covered land that uses wind, solar, or geothermal energy to make electricity, or transmits electricity to support such projects, and may include energy storage. Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior.

sections.title: Section 2 Updating National Goals for Renewable Energy Production on Federal Land
sections.text: This section changes an existing law called the Energy Act of two thousand twenty. It increases the goal for renewable energy production on federal land from twenty five gigawatts to sixty gigawatts. It also moves the deadline from two thousand twenty five to December thirty first, two thousand thirty. The section also requires the Secretary of the Interior to work with the Secretary of Agriculture and other federal agency leaders to update the national goals for renewable energy on federal land within eighteen months of this law being passed.

sections.title: Section 3 Land Use Planning and Updates to Programmatic Environmental Impact Statements
sections.text: This section requires the Secretary to identify areas on covered land where people can apply for renewable energy projects and to consider setting up priority areas for these projects. When doing this work, the Secretary must follow multiple use principles and work toward the national renewable energy goals. Applications for renewable energy projects in priority areas get special treatment including faster processing and the chance to participate in regional mitigation plans. The Secretary must establish priority areas for solar energy projects within eighteen months and start reviewing wind energy planning within one year, finishing that review within three years. At least every ten years, the Secretary must review all land allocations for renewable energy projects after getting public comments. This review looks at encouraging new projects while protecting wildlife habitat, endangered species, water resources, cultural resources, recreation, wilderness areas, specially managed lands, and areas important to tribes. The Secretary can add, change, or remove priority areas and exclusion areas based on these reviews. If it looks like national renewable energy goals might not be met, the Secretary must work more frequently to designate new areas for renewable energy projects. The section also requires updating three major environmental impact statements for geothermal, solar, and wind energy from two thousand eight, two thousand twelve, and two thousand five respectively. Nothing in this section changes the requirement for specific environmental reviews of individual projects. When developing updates, the Secretary must coordinate with state, tribal, and local governments, transmission companies, energy developers, and others to make sure priority areas consider economic viability, environmental impacts, feasibility of using previously disturbed land, and consistency with federal land management laws. The Secretary must also determine if there is adequate transmission for renewable energy projects and review existing land use plans if more transmission is needed. The Secretary may create incentives for renewable energy projects in priority areas.

sections.title: Section 4 Improving Wind and Solar Energy Project Permitting
sections.text: This section makes changes to how wind and solar energy project applications are processed. It allows the Secretary to give State Renewable Energy Coordination Offices the authority to process applications for eligible projects on Bureau of Land Management land in their state. These office managers can issue grants or leases, use other Bureau employees or hire additional experts to help with applications, and must report to the State Director of the applicable Bureau office. The Secretary cannot give this authority to employees of local field or district offices except as assistants. Within thirty days of receiving a complete application for a wind or solar energy right of way, the Secretary must provide a cost recovery agreement. Once this agreement is issued and fees are paid, no new claims can be made on that land while the application is active. Applications must address any known conflicts with other land uses, but no additional studies are required to consider an application complete. If an environmental impact statement is needed, the Secretary must issue a notice of intent within one hundred eighty days of notifying the applicant that their application is complete, unless the Secretary determines an extension is needed due to low priority status, project specific circumstances, or the application not meeting approval requirements. The Secretary may create regulations allowing preliminary geotechnical work and weather monitoring for renewable energy projects to be categorically excluded from detailed environmental review requirements. When processing applications, the Secretary must give priority to projects in priority areas and process other applications in the order they were received. The Secretary cannot use a competitive process for reviewing applications except when two or more applicants file for the same site within fifteen days of each other, or as established by future rulemaking. This limitation does not apply to competitive right of way leases in priority areas.

sections.title: Section 5 Increasing Economic Certainty
sections.text: This section sets rules for rents and fees charged for renewable energy projects on federal land. The total rental rates and fees cannot exceed the average amount charged for similar activities on private land in the same state or county. The Secretary does not have to conduct individual appraisals and may use average cash rents from the National Agricultural Statistics Service's Pastureland Rents Survey based on the five year period ending when the rental rate is determined. Once a base rental rate is established, any increases are limited to the Implicit Price Deflator Gross Product Index published by the Bureau of Economic Analysis on the date the lease or right of way was issued. The Secretary may only charge capacity fees if such fees are charged on state or private land in the same region or state. For bonds required for decommissioning renewable energy projects, the Secretary must adopt a process that does not set a minimum per acre amount and bases bond requirements on the difference between estimated site specific net costs of land reclamation and the salvage value of materials available after the project is decommissioned.

sections.title: Section 6 Disposition of Revenues and Renewable Energy Resource Conservation Fund
sections.text: This section explains how money collected from wind and solar energy projects gets distributed. From January first, two thousand twenty six through December thirty first, two thousand forty five, twenty five percent goes to the state where the revenue comes from, twenty five percent goes to the counties where the revenue comes from based on the percentage of land involved, fifteen percent goes to the Bureau of Land Management's Renewable Energy Management account to help process permits, and thirty five percent goes to a new Renewable Energy Resource Conservation Fund. Starting January first, two thousand forty six, the percentages change to twenty five percent to states, twenty five percent to counties, ten percent to permit processing, and forty percent to the Fund. For projects in multiple states, the Secretary must create a rule within one hundred eighty days explaining how to split the state payments. Application filing fees are not subject to this revenue sharing and can be kept by the agency to cover costs. Money paid to states and counties must be used consistent with existing mineral leasing laws, and county payments are in addition to existing payments in lieu of taxes. The law creates a Renewable Energy Resource Conservation Fund managed by the Secretary. This Fund can provide money to federal, state, local, and tribal agencies in regions with renewable energy projects on federal land for restoring and protecting fish and wildlife habitat and corridors, protecting wetlands and waterways, and preserving and improving recreational access to federal land and water. The Secretary can enter cooperative agreements with state and tribal agencies, nonprofit organizations, and other entities to carry out these activities. Money in the Fund earns interest based on current Treasury rates, and this interest can be used without further appropriation. Each year, the Secretary must report to Congress about money collected, payments made, and remaining Fund balance. Congress intends that Fund revenues supplement rather than replace annual appropriations for these conservation activities.

sections.title: Section 7 Savings Clause
sections.text: This section clarifies that despite everything else in this law, the Secretary of the Interior and Secretary of Agriculture must continue to manage public land under the principles of multiple use and sustained yield as required by existing federal land management laws. This applies to land use planning, permit processing, and conducting environmental reviews.
